h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Then the bar is three eighth-note beats, not three generic places. Is that the point, or does the tile still only mark the count?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was on track to confirm the separation of top and bottom box jobs by repeating the quarter-note example with increasing fluency.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: sets three blank tiles up to the bar line, puts the eighth-note card in the lower box, and draws an eighth note on each tile
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner physically enacts the new representation — blank tiles for count, drawn note shapes for beat-unit value — completing the device T3 introduced and demonstrating that the two jobs of the tile have been separated in practice.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs. Use the blank side for beat places only: keep three places before the bar line, put the eighth-note card on the lower box, then draw the note shape each blank place is now claiming.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly names the failure of the prior route ('the tile route has stopped settling it because the tile was carrying two jobs') and introduces a structurally different device — blank-side tiles plus drawn note shapes — that separates count from note-value in a way the face-up tile representation could not, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use the blank side for beat places only: keep three places before the bar line, put the eighth-note card on the lower box, then draw the note shape each blank place is now claiming.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The device is precisely fitted to the learner's blockage — tiles carrying two jobs — by physically stripping one job (note shape) from the tile and relocating it to a learner-drawn mark, giving the learner an actionable, transferable criterion that is visibly different from the failed route and requires the learner to perform the separation rather than observe it.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Blank tile first marks the count; the lower box gives each counted place its note value. Mark the bracket as the bar and each tile as an eighth-note beat: three eighth-note beats in this bar.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 responds directly to L4's question about whether the tile 'still only marks the count' by explicitly articulating the two-step sequence (blank tile = count, lower box = note value) and naming the result in L4's own terms ('three eighth-note beats'), showing uptake of the learner's revised framing rather than merely continuing the prior lesson plan.

scored.recoheredEarlier: the arrow still looks like cart-goes-right once the card is down
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue placing cards and naming objects one at a time, following the established object-first protocol without distinguishing force types.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: sets the track card in the slot, keeping the strip over the arrowheads … 'Track on cart. For the support contact, the track pushes up on the cart, so this arrow should point up when the strip lifts. If this card is also supposed to cover rubbing, that needs a separate sideways arrow.'
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner not only executes the new strip-gated protocol correctly but spontaneously applies a new criterion — distinguishing normal force from friction as requiring separate arrows — showing the force-identification task has moved beyond simple card placement.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The card named the first force. The arrowhead is still trying to read as cart motion. This strip stays down until the sentence passes one test: other object on cart. Put track in the slot. Say: track on cart. Then lift the strip and choose the direction for that named interaction.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 names the old route's failure (arrowhead still reads as cart motion even after card placement) and introduces a new physical constraint — the blank strip blocking arrowheads — that makes the sentence-gate test a visible, enacted condition rather than a verbal reminder, satisfying both stock-taking contrast and new public mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: This strip stays down until the sentence passes one test: other object on cart. Put track in the slot. Say: track on cart. Then lift the strip and choose the direction for that named interaction.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The strip device is precisely fitted to the diagnosed blockage (premature direction-reading), gives the learner a concrete, self-administrable gate with a single testable criterion, and is visibly different from the prior verbal naming protocol, though it remains tutor-installed rather than learner-generated.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot L3, so no recognition-contingent uptake can be scored.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was converging on an accepted wording for the metadata note and appeared ready to close the check cleanly.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Slot A. Release-note sentence: `rainfall_mm` is the field label for the rainfall amount, in millimeters, recorded on a city-month row.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new slot-classification device and produces a release-note sentence that meets the gate criterion, completing a task that did not exist before T3 introduced it.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The `42` check settled the value; it did not settle the header. Switch to the release-note gate: put `rainfall_mm` in one slot only. Slot A: field label, naming the kind of value a row records in that column. Slot B: column claim, asserting a result about the column's rows. Mark A or B, then write one release-note sentence a data user could not mistake for Slot B.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly marks the old route (value-check) as insufficient for the live problem (header ambiguity) and introduces a new binary-slot classification device plus a release-note sentence gate, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Switch to the release-note gate: put `rainfall_mm` in one slot only. Slot A: field label, naming the kind of value a row records in that column. Slot B: column claim, asserting a result about the column's rows. Mark A or B, then write one release-note sentence a data user could not mistake for Slot B.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The slot device is precisely fitted to the learner's stated confusion (label vs. claim), gives a binary actionable criterion, and the release-note sentence gate adds a transferable production constraint; it is slightly over-directed in pre-labelling both slots, which limits learner agency marginally.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot turn L3 that responds to L4's revised framing; the transcript ends after L4.
